videos.title: Mistakes American Tourist Make in England
videos.description:
Whether you are heading to Lisbon, Bournmouth, Bristol, Manchester or Newcastle there are a lot of do's and some really important don'ts of visiting England and we want to make sure you don't make any tourist mistakes when you visit England. So here we have the most common mistakes tourists make when they go to England on vacation. Honest travel advice for visiting England.
Filmed in York, Liverpool, and Chester, England, UK

videos.transcript: Hey, fellow travelers, Mark here with Tourist World. Today, we're here in Chester, England, and today we'll talk about are some of the mistakes that travelers make when they do come here to England because this is a beautiful country. There's so much to see and do outside of just going to London that a lot of people miss out on. And we all make mistakes when we travel. We want to make sure that you don't make mistakes when you do come here. And I think one mistake actually just comes with timing. One of the things I see the most troubles with the tourists have when they come here is the idea of half seven. If someone says half seven to an American, they're like, "What? Half of seven is three and a half." But the thing is, when you're telling time in England, half seven means 7:30, okay? Now, if you're in Germany or some other place, half seven actually means 6:30. [music] But here, half seven is 7:30. That's where I've seen a lot of miscommunication with tourists and travelers when it comes to making reservations for restaurants or train tickets. So, remember, half seven is 7:30, okay? Now, another mistake that tourists make when they go around England is they forget to take advantage of the deals cuz there's so many deals you can get, whether it's on train tickets, it's going out to eat, getting the extra specials. There's all kinds of stuff. [music] Heck, not using the National Trust when you're going around to see the stately manners of the the the homes around the country. There's so many places that put together deals where if you buy a pass for a few days, it will really pay for itself. So, really make sure you're doing your research on how you can save some money when you come here cuz honestly, I know every time we come here, we get our National Trust pass and it helps us get into a bunch of different stately homes and manor houses and all kinds of things. And that really helps save our budget and we get to see more when we're there cuz we're not worried about well, how much is the next place going to cost? How much is the next place going to cost? It all kind of goes together. So, make sure you look for those things. And kind of going off of the National Trust is also I see tourists make the mistake of not staying in historic inns and bed and breakfasts. Look, when you're going around, if you're in a city like this here in Chester, there's so many historic inns you can go stay in. Place I'm staying with has been an inn since the 1500s. Yes, the steps are a little wobbly and they're they're kind of wonky and stuff like that, but the building's like 500 years old. What do you expect? And that's one of the things that can really make this more of a cultural adventure when you're here and to really get a better vibe and better feel for everything [music] is staying at those historic inns, staying at those bed and breakfasts, and not just going to a standard hotel. Now, you can, there's nothing wrong with that, but if you're going to be going to these villages, you're going to need to look for and and towns, you're going to be to looking for local accommodation because there's not always a chain hotel in every single town you go to or city you want to go to in England. So, make sure you're a little bit more open-minded with your accommodation options. Now, another thing people say that keeps them from coming to England is they think it's it's going to be a very expensive trip and and yes, if you're going to go to London, London will be a very expensive trip. I will not deny that. That's going to be one of the most expensive cities you can go to as a traveler. But here's the thing, if you come up here to the north of England, you go other places in the south of England, or the Midlands, prices are significantly less than you go to London. And so, don't think that oh, England is going to be a super expensive vacation. No, it's not. I know for me, I've spent 2 weeks between York, Liverpool, and Chester, and I've spent the same amount of money as 1 week in London, okay? So, kind of think about that when you're looking at where you're going to go because choosing where you go in the country can have a big impact on your budget when you come here. Now, another thing I see tourists making a mistake about when they come here to England is not respecting the queue culture. [music] Look, line culture, queue culture, standing in line is very much a thing here. And in my video on how to upset the locals, you know, kind of piss them off when you're here, [music] that's like number one of the things that will upset people. And you have to realize is if you come to a place where everyone kind of bum rushes the door when the subway opens or the train opens, or whoever can just get in front of people gets to go first, that's not how it works here. People very much set up a nice line, nice queue, they wait in line, they don't complain about it, they wait there, you know, stiff upper lip, we're just going to wait until it's our turn. That's how it is here. And if you go against that, people are not going to be out with you. They're going to say something to you and and and sometimes you don't realize it cuz maybe you're from hey, whoever gets to the front is to the front. Doesn't matter if there's a line or not, but that is a mistake I see a lot of international travelers making here. Another mistake that tourists make is sometimes they don't realize the differences between England, Scotland, Wales, Britain, Great Britain, the United Kingdom, the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland. You know, you have all these different words you'll hear about talking about different places around here. I think it's really important you realize that if somebody's from England, they're English. If they're from Wales, they're Welsh. They're not English. If they're from Scotland, they're a Scot. They're not English, okay? And so that's one thing that tourists don't always know and they sometimes upset people because if you call an Englishman a Scot, there's a connotation to that. If you call a Scottish person English, there's a connotation to that as well. So, you do want to be careful with that cuz that's just a simple mistake that tourists do make. And another thing is when you're traveling around, a lot of people make the mistake of I'm only going to stay in cities and I'm only going to stay in towns when I come here to England. And I got to tell you, there's a lot of natural beauty when [music] you come here, whether you're going to the Yorkshire Dales or you're going to go to the Peak District or you're going to go to the Lake [music] District, which is just gorgeous. See the Cliffs of Dover. Going around, you have to realize there's tons of like uh hiking trails. There's tons of walking trails. There's all kinds of nature you can have when you're here when you come to England. It's not just going from city to city to city. So, let's hop to another part of England to uh talk about some more mistakes that tourists make. So, now we're here in York, outside of York Minster. And my next mistake that tourists make when they come to England is they think that it rains all the time every day when you're here in England. And that's not true. I've been here a number of times in the summer where I was was literally for 10 days and it didn't rain at all. Like, it was sunny, I got sunburn. I mean, you don't think about sunburn in England when you come here, but it does happen. So, don't think it rains all the time. Now, I know it's cloudy now, but just be aware that it's not a torrential downpour every single day when you're here in England, okay? Now, having said that, I I do want to say another mistake is not bringing rain gear when you come because yes, it probably will rain sometime when you're here. I won't lie to you. I remember I was here for New Year's, it was like raining sideways, and my socks were wet. It was crazy time. But, have yourself. Like, my backpack, I have a rain poncho in there in case it does rain. So, do be prepared for that. But, don't think it's going to rain all the time. But, just be prepared in case it does. Now, the next costly mistake I see tourists make when they come to England is they disrespect or disregard the coins. Look, the coins you get when you come here, they're actually worth something. It's not like in the US where even if you have like a lot of coins, it might be, you know, 82 cents, but you have a handful of coins. Here, if you have a handful of coins, you could probably pay for dinner cuz you got maybe a £2 coin or £1 coin. I mean, 50 cent piece. I mean, money coins really go farther here than in like in the US and other places. So, just be aware of that. And don't just leave them behind or throw them in the fountain. Use them to buy some cool souvenirs or buy yourself a snack or buy yourself dinner, which I've done a number of times with the coins I've had here. And when you are ordering, another mistake I see tourists make is um they order an ale and they expect a beer. Look, when you get an ale here in England, they actually pull it. It's like a pump. They're actually pulling it to get it out, all right? And you have those this ales when you're here, and they're very different than your you know, your Bud Light or your Budweiser or your Stella Artois, that's that bubbly cold ice cold beer that that tickles your nose. No, ales are very different than your traditional kind of beer you kind of think of when you're thinking of getting a beer with your buddies and crack off a top and do this. No, they're going to pull that beer, okay? So, when you order an ale, don't expect a beer, expect an ale. It is a different experience. And yes, that means it's not going to be ice cold. It's going to be close to room temperature than ice cold, but it's still going to be fantastic. Now, the next mistake I see tourists making is they think London is England. Yes, London is in England, and London has a lot of stuff that's in England, but don't [music] think just because you went to London, you saw England. There's a reason why I'm filming this in York and other places around England, so I can show you this country has so much more than just London. It's like saying I went to New York City and I saw the US. I went to Paris and I saw France. I went to Tokyo, I saw Japan. There's so much more throughout England you can go and enjoy. Here in the north when you're going to York, it's a great time. Or or maybe you're going to go over to the Liverpool and see the where the Beatles were. Or maybe you go down to Brighton or you go to the Cliffs of Dover. I mean, you go around England, there's so much more to do here than doing London. So please, even if you're only going to be going to London, do some of the day trips to like, you know, go down to Brighton, go to Canterbury, go to Bath, go see some of the other parts because don't think that it's just London you're here. That's a mistake a lot of people make. Go and explore this country cuz it is beautiful. And when you're going to explore this beautiful country, another mistake I see tourists make is they get scared off of driving because they drive on a different side of the road than you drive in back home, so they're scared to drive around. Look, it's okay to be scared driving in London cuz even the locals are scared to drive in London, but driving around England is wonderful. You can go and explore so much more, see more castles, see more stately homes, see, you know, the different national parks around here. There's so many great things to see and do that you need to have a car to truly enjoy. And so many tourists make the mistake of, "Yeah, I guess the other side of the road, I roundabouts scare me. I don't want to do this." No, don't make that mistake. You'll be okay. The first roundabout or two will scare you a little bit, but after that, you'll be fine. And you'll be surprised when you get away from London how little traffic you're going to have, so you can enjoy it and not freak out about all the driving and all the other side of the road stuff. And when people actually do start driving around England, they start to realize another mistake they made. And that is thinking that English food is just fish and chips and bangers and mash and other pub grub kind of stuff. Look, England has a lot of immigration from all over the world that's come here and they've brought their food with them. So, you could have fantastic food that is not just straight from a fry-up. You could have so much great food when you're here that's not just an English breakfast or something like that. You could have non-English food when you're here and have a lot of great food as well. And it's not just that bangers and mash, fish and chips, and hitting the pub for a grub. No, there's a lot more than the chippy when you do come here. And with that food, another mistake I see tourists make is when they go to a pub, they think people are going to come take their order. Look, if you're going to a pub and you see a number on your table, realize that probably no one's going to come and take your order. You need to go to the bar to put your order in and then you tell them what your table number is and then someone will bring it out to you, okay? Cuz I've been in a number of pubs I mean a number of pubs in England over the years where tourists are just sitting there going, "Well, no one's come to serve us. What's going on here?" Look, don't make that mistake. Just go up, order at the bar, and you'll be fine. Just take your number up so you can pay and they know where to bring it, okay? Now, if you're going to be traveling by train around England, which is super easy to do, another thing I have to say, another mistake I see people make is they don't get a rail card. Look, if you get a rail card, you can get some serious discounts on train purchases because the trains in England and going around Great Britain overall, they can be a little pricey and saving some money just by having a rail card can go a long way. So, if you're going to be coming here for like 10 days, you're going from London to York to Liverpool to Chester, you know, to Wales to up to Scotland wherever, those rail cards can be a big discount for you and help you save a lot of money. So, don't be afraid to buy one even if you're only here for a few days because honestly, it can really save you some really good money and pay for itself. So, another mistake tourists make when they come here is really trying to understand the kind of love I'm not going to say love-hate. I'm going to say love/loathe relationship that England and English fans have with the England football team, the three lions. I mean, you'll be surprised how much if you listen to the radio, how much they love England, how England really should win every World Cup and every European Championship and every Champions League and every UEFA Cup, everything ever. England should win every single soccer football tournament ever ever ever ever. And you'll hear them like just they'll It's It's crazy how much like we are the best. It's coming home. It's coming home. Like you have this crazy mentality have for the English team. Don't make the mistake that you think you can understand it because you can't. And that's okay. So let's go to a different place to continue on our mistakes that tourists make when they come here to England. Now we're here in Liverpool and I think the next mistake I want to talk about is when people come to England they think when it says an 85% chance of rain that it's going to be a complete downpour the entire day. Look, the rain here is just kind of like rain. It just kind of comes and goes during the day. It's usually not a really heavy downpour. Yes, it does happen sometimes, but it's more of just like a kind of constant on and off rain. Yes, there's some dark clouds over there, but across the way it's all sunny. So don't make the mistake of thinking that the weather's going to be one way or not or that downpours are going to come if they say it's going to rain. It's more of a a light constant drizzle most of the time, all right? Now since I'm here in Liverpool, I think it's important to mention this mistake, wearing a Manchester United jersey here in Liverpool. Well, not just that, but I think a lot of tourists think that, "Oh, I'm going to go to England. I'll get my new t-shirt, my new jersey for my favorite team, whether it's Manchester, Liverpool, Chelsea, whatever, and then they're just going to wear it out when they go to the pub, when they go around and see the sights." And And I think that's a mistake that tourists make because most of the time it doesn't matter. People don't really care. But, if it's like a game day, or people are getting a little bit too exuberant, or the lads have had a few much too many drinks, that can be something you want to think about. So, get your jersey when you're here. If you're going to go to the game, go ahead and wear it. But, if you're not, save it until you get home. Now, another mistake I see tourists make when they come to England is they have the wrong adapter. When you come here, you want an adapter like this. See how it's these three flat things here? That's what you want to have. That's what you're looking for for the UK in general, okay? So, if you're looking to get an adapter, I recommend you get like a universal adapter that'll have like Europe, and it'll have the US, and then it'll have the UK. So, make sure you have the right adapter. Now, a mistake that even seasoned travelers sometimes make when they come to England is, if you go to Europe, or you or you know, mainland Europe, you're in Germany, or Spain, or or you're in the US even, or in Canada, you're used to having just one company run all the train lines. And that's a mistake you make when you come here to England because there's literally, it feels like dozens of different companies that actually run the train lines depending where you're going to go throughout the country. And so, when you go online, you go to the National Rail website, and you you go to book your ticket, and then it kicks you off to another website, people are like, "Oh, is this a scam?" No, they're going to send you to the website of the company that runs the train line that you want to get. So, for example, I'm here for a 12-day trip. I've had to sign up for four different programs on four different train lines to get myself around the country. So, don't think there's just one train line when you're here. And another mistake, and it doesn't matter if you're at the the railway station, or you're at the bus station, or you're at a museum, or you're walking around the city, is that's when tourists don't ask for assistance when they're here in England because they have a great system here to help out tourists and help out people, but also the people here are pretty friendly and they will help you out. I mean, and it doesn't matter if you're lost in London or lost in Leeds, someone will help you if you ask. And the thing is, the police are very helpful here, museum guides are helpful here, your concierges are here, or just a dude walking down the street with a purse you're sitting by on the train, you can talk to them. I mean, here in Liverpool, the people are more than outgoing and give you advice if you look lost. They'll be like, "Hey mate, you lost?" You know, so so don't be afraid to ask for help when you're here. And you're going to maybe want to ask that help, especially if you want to go shopping late, cuz I think that's a that's a mistake that tourists make when they come to England. They think, "Oh, it's going to be open late night. I can go shopping in the evening. It'll be great." No. I mean, that is a big mistake you make when you come here. You need to realize, if you're in London, yeah, the shops stay open longer, right? And the museums might stay open longer. But, if you're here in the north, you're in the Midlands or or wherever you're going to be going around, you have to realize that some places the museums might close at 4:00. The stores might close at 5:00 or 6:00 p.m. I mean, that's one of the things you have to kind of think about when you're here is, "Hey, when do I need to get everything done?" So, make sure you're looking for the schedules of all the places you want to visit, so you can make sure you can get in, cuz you don't want to miss out on going to some of the great museums that are here or the historic homes, all right?

videos.title: This Is Why You Need To Visit Newcastle Upon Tyne | England's Most Underrated City
videos.description:
Welcome to my Newcastle upon Tyne VLOG, I'm proud to be bringing you to my home! Join me as I explore some of the most iconic landmarks and hidden gems that this city has to offer.

We'll start by strolling along the iconic Quayside, with its famous bridges and scenic riverside views. Then, we'll walk through the charming streets of the city center, taking in the stunning Georgian and Victorian architecture, including the famous Grey Street and Grainger Town. As a season ticket holder of Newcastle United, I'll also give you a sneak peak of St James' Park on a match day, and a glimpse of what all us Geordies are passionate about. To finish off on our first day exploring, we'll also visit a few of the free museums such as the Discovery Museum, The Hancock Museum and the Laing Art Gallery.

Day 2 brings a lovely little surprise of snowfall, and we begin at Grey's Monument, this time working our way through Grainger Town, and passing the bustling Grainger Market, where you can find a wide array of local products and delicious food. A quick stop off at The Bigg Market, then we head towards the grand Central Station, then into a hidden gem, the Lit and Phil.

I'll then take you into St Nicholas' Cathedral, which I had never been in before, and then along to Newcastle's Castle Keep, which gave Newcastle its name.

As promised, finishing off with a lovely surprise, we'll be heading down into the dark with Victoria Tunnel Tours. I found exploring the Victoria Tunnel fascinating, and really enjoyed the stories and history alongside it.

It took a lot of planning, researching, and editing to finally get this video out, so I really hope you enjoyed it and it wasn't too boring. If you all enjoy this video, I may very well make a part 2, picking up on some stuff I missed out, but also including some other things to see and do which are slightly outside of the city.

A huge thanks to Scott Buckley for his incredible compositions: https://youtube.com/c/ScottBuckley

videos.title: NEWCASTLE City Guide | England | Travel Guide
videos.description:
Newcastle upon Tyne, or simply Newcastle, is a city and metropolitan borough in Tyne and Wear, England. Newcastle is located on the River Tyne's northern bank and is a beloved travel destination for tourists. Newcastle is also the most populous city of North East England. Famous landmarks in Newcastle include the Tyne Bridge, the Millennium Bridge, the Newcastle Castle, the Grey's Monument and the and the Millennium Bridge. Newcastle is also famed for its nightlife and Newcastle United Football Club.

videos.transcript: Welcome to Newcastle! This vibrant and dynamic city is a must-see destination for anyone traveling to England. Each year, millions of tourists visit Newcastle, drawn by its rich history, beautiful architecture and a lively cultural scene. Newcastle is located in the northeast of England in the county of Tyne and Wear. It sits on the north bank of the river Tyne and is surrounded by beautiful countryside and gorgeous coastline. The city is situated just a few miles from the North Sea, which has played a vital role in Newcastle's history. the Northumberland National Park and the rugged coastline of Northumberland are just a short drive away. The city is home to over 300 000 people, making it the most populous city in the northeast of England. The locals are known as Geordies, and are famous for their friendly and welcoming nature. Newcastle is a city steeped in rich history and culture that dates back to the Roman era. Originally known as Pons Aelius, the settlement was established by the Roman emperor Hadrian. The city was strategically placed at the northernmost point of the Roman Empire, serving as a key military stronghold. Over the centuries, Newcastle evolved into a thriving hub for trade and commerce, thanks in part to its location on the river Tyne. The city grew rapidly during the Industrial Revolution, becoming a center for coal mining, shipbuilding and engineering. Despite the challenges of the 20th century, Newcastle persevered and underwent a major revitalization in the 1990s, leading to the development of new cultural and entertainment venues that have transformed the city into a modern and vibrant destination. Today, Newcastle is a thriving city that celebrates its rich heritage, while embracing the possibilities of the future. One of the main draws for visitors is the Quayside, a bustling waterfront area that offers a unique blend of history, culture and entertainment. At the heart of the Quayside is the river Tyne, which has played a significant role in the city's history and development. Tyne is a popular spot for leisure boats and river cruises, offering visitors the chance to take in the city's skyline from the water. Quayside is also known for its vibrant nightlife and food scene. There are countless bars, restaurants and cafes along the river. The area is particularly popular with students, thanks to its proximity to Newcastle University and Northumbria University. As you wander through the streets of Newcastle, it's hard not to notice the striking landmark that towers above the city - the Tyne Bridge. This iconic structure is not only an impressive feat of engineering, but also a symbol of Newcastle's proud industrial past. The Tyne Bridge spans the river Tyne, connecting Newcastle and Gateshead, and is considered one of the most beautiful bridges in the world. Designed by the engineering firm Mott, Hay and Anderson, the bridge was completed in 1928 and has since become a symbol of Newcastle's industrial heritage. The Millennium Bridge is a pedestrian and cycle-only bridge that spans across the river Tyne. It was designed by the renowned architect Wilkinson Eyre, and was completed in 2001, just in time for the turn of the millennium. As you approach the bridge, you can't help but be struck by its sleek, modern design. The bridge is made up of two graceful arches that curve high above the water, creating a striking silhouette against the skyline. But what makes this bridge truly special is the way it moves. The bridge is designed to pivot in the middle, allowing boats and ships to pass underneath. It's a marvel of engineering and a testament to human ingenuity. With its towering spires and beautiful stonework, the Newcastle Cathedral is a true masterpiece of Gothic design. Built over the course of centuries, its origins can be traced back to the 12th century, when a small church was first constructed on this site. Over time, this modest building was expanded and transformed, taking on the grandeur and majesty that we see today. Inside, visitors can explore the complex carvings and artwork that adorn the walls and ceilings of this church, marveling at the skill and craftsmanship of the artisans who created them. The cathedral is also a hub of cultural activity, hosting concerts, lectures and other events that bring people from all walks of life together. The Jesmond Dene Park is a true oasis, nestled in the heart of Newcastle. This public park is a hidden gem that provides a much needed escape from the hustle and bustle of city life. Its history dates back to the 19th century, when it was privately owned by the Armstrong family. In 1883, Lord Armstrong gifted the park to the people of Newcastle, and it has been a public park ever since. The park was designed in a naturalistic style and features a mix of woodland, meadows and a stream that runs through the center of the park. Jesmond Dene Park is a popular destination for nature lovers, joggers and dog walkers, and has a network of trails that wind through the woods, offering visitors the chance to explore the park's natural beauty. The Discovery Museum in Newcastle is a fantastic place for tourists. The museum was established in 1934 and features a wide range of displays, including exhibitions on science and industry, maritime history and local life. With over 250 000 items in its collection, the Discovery Museum is a treasure trove of fascinating artifacts. One of the highlights of the museum is the Turbinia, a ship that was once the fastest vessel in the world. Visitors can explore the ship and learn about its incredible history and technological innovations. There are also exhibits on Newcastle's coal-mining industry, the history of the local railways, and even an interactive area for children. if you loved this video, hit the like button and subscribe. Newcastle, England is an amazing place to visit in England. If you want to know top 10 what to do in Newcastle or you need a travel guide, please keep watching.

5. Enjoy a football match at St James' Park: A visit to St James' Park in Newcastle, England is an exhilarating experience for football enthusiasts. Witnessing a football match here allows you to immerse yourself in the passion and energy of the game, surrounded by devoted fans. It's an opportunity to create lasting memories and feel the undeniable thrill of live football action.

4. Explore the Victoria Tunnel: Explore the Victoria Tunnel in Newcastle, England, for an intriguing journey into history. This unique underground attraction offers a captivating glimpse into the city's past as a crucial hub for coal mining and World War II. As you walk through the dark tunnels, guided by knowledgeable experts, you'll uncover stories of industry, air raids, and resilience, immersing yourself in a rich tapestry of Newcastle's heritage.

3. Visit the Discovery Museum: The Discovery Museum in Newcastle, England is a must-visit destination for history and science enthusiasts. It offers a captivating journey through the city's industrial past, showcasing exhibits on maritime heritage, engineering innovations, and local inventions. Visitors can engage with interactive displays, explore hands-on workshops, and even step aboard historic vessels. With its rich educational experience and engaging displays, the Discovery Museum guarantees an enlightening and enjoyable visit.

2. Enjoy the Quayside Market:The Quayside Market in Newcastle, England is a must-visit destination for its vibrant and eclectic atmosphere. With a stunning riverside location, it offers a delightful blend of local produce, delicious street food, unique crafts, and live entertainment. Immerse yourself in the bustling energy of the market, discover hidden gems, and indulge in the flavors and creativity that make this experience truly unforgettable.

1. Take a walk in Jesmond Dene Park: Jesmond Dene Park in Newcastle, England, offers a serene and enchanting escape from the city's bustling energy. Its lush greenery, meandering pathways, and picturesque waterfalls create a tranquil atmosphere that soothes the soul.
